facts.text: The FIFA World Cup is the most-watched sporting event in the world, with the 2022 final drawing an estimated 1.5 billion viewers.
Pelé scored 1,283 career goals and is the only player to win three FIFA World Cup titles (1958, 1962, 1970).
A regulation soccer field is between 100-130 yards long and 50-100 yards wide, with goals measuring 8 feet high and 24 feet wide.
Lionel Messi has won the Ballon d'Or (world's best player) a record 8 times.
Cristiano Ronaldo is the all-time leading scorer in men's international soccer with over 130 goals for Portugal.
The English Premier League is the most-watched sports league in the world, broadcast in 212 territories.
The first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay in 1930, with the host nation defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final.
Brazil has won the most World Cup titles with 5 championships (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002).
The offside rule, one of soccer's most debated laws, was introduced in 1863 and has been modified multiple times since.
Manchester United is one of the most valuable sports franchises in the world, worth over $6 billion.
The UEFA Champions League final is the most-watched annual club sporting event worldwide.
Diego Maradona's 'Hand of God' goal against England in the 1986 World Cup is one of the most controversial goals in soccer history.
Maradona also scored the 'Goal of the Century' in the same 1986 match, dribbling past five English players and the goalkeeper.
A standard soccer ball has 32 panels and weighs between 14-16 ounces.
The MLS (Major League Soccer) was founded in 1993 and began play in 1996 as part of the United States' bid to host the 1994 World Cup.
Marta, the Brazilian forward, is considered the greatest female soccer player of all time and has scored more World Cup goals than any woman.
The 'Beautiful Game' is a nickname for soccer, popularized by Pelé.
Real Madrid has won the UEFA Champions League/European Cup a record 15 times.
AC Milan, Barcelona, and Bayern Munich are among soccer's most storied clubs with multiple Champions League titles.
The 'Cruyff Turn' was a revolutionary move invented by Johan Cruyff during the 1974 World Cup.
VAR (Video Assistant Referee) was introduced to major soccer competitions starting in 2018 to reduce officiating errors.
The longest soccer match ever played was a charity game in 2016 that lasted 108 hours.
Zinedine Zidane headbutted Marco Materazzi in the 2006 World Cup Final, ending his career with a red card.
The USWNT (U.S. Women's National Team) has won 4 FIFA Women's World Cup titles, more than any other nation.
Megan Rapinoe won the Golden Boot and Golden Ball at the 2019 Women's World Cup while also becoming an outspoken advocate for equality.
Germany has won 4 FIFA World Cups (1954, 1974, 1990, 2014) and is one of soccer's most consistently successful nations.
The 'Rabona' is a trick shot where a player wraps their kicking foot behind their standing leg to strike the ball.
Liverpool's legendary 2005 Champions League Final comeback from 3-0 down against AC Milan is known as 'The Miracle of Istanbul.'
A soccer player runs an average of 7 miles per game, with midfielders covering the most ground.
The 'El Clásico' rivalry between Real Madrid and Barcelona is one of the most intense rivalries in all of sports.
Neymar's transfer from Barcelona to Paris Saint-Germain in 2017 for €222 million was the most expensive transfer in soccer history.
The 'Bicycle Kick' (scissor kick or overhead kick) is one of soccer's most spectacular and difficult moves.
Argentina won the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, with Lionel Messi finally capturing the trophy in what many consider the greatest final ever.
The penalty shootout was introduced to FIFA competitions in 1970 to replace the coin toss method of deciding tied matches.
Erling Haaland scored 36 Premier League goals in his debut season with Manchester City in 2022-23, breaking the single-season record.
The 'GOAT debate' in soccer primarily centers around Lionel Messi and Cristiano Ronaldo, two players who dominated for nearly two decades.
The 'Tifo' displays created by soccer fan groups are massive choreographed visual displays using banners, flags, and cards.
Italy's 'Catenaccio' defensive system, emphasizing strong defense and counterattacking, defined Italian soccer for decades.
The FIFA Ballon d'Or has been awarded since 1956 to the world's best footballer.
Kylian Mbappé became the second teenager to score in a World Cup Final when he netted twice in the 2018 final.
The 'Panenka' penalty is a chipped shot down the middle of the goal, named after Antonín Panenka who used it in the 1976 European Championship Final.
Barcelona's 'Tiki-Taka' playing style emphasized short passing and movement, dominating world soccer under Pep Guardiola from 2008-2012.
The 'Maracanazo' refers to Uruguay's shocking 2-1 upset of Brazil in the 1950 World Cup Final in Rio de Janeiro.
Soccer boots (cleats) have evolved from heavy leather work boots to lightweight synthetic shoes weighing under 7 ounces.
The 'Golden Goal' rule — ending a match when a goal was scored in extra time — was used from 1993 to 2004 then abolished.
Alex Morgan, a U.S. women's soccer star, has scored over 120 international goals and became a global icon for women's sports.
The Bundesliga in Germany is known for having the highest average attendance of any soccer league in the world.
Iker Casillas was nicknamed 'San Iker' (Saint Iker) for his legendary shot-stopping ability at Real Madrid and with Spain.
The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be hosted jointly by the United States, Mexico, and Canada with an expanded 48-team format.
Total Football, pioneered by the Netherlands in the 1970s, featured every outfield player being able to play any position.
Lev Yashin, the 'Black Spider,' is considered the greatest goalkeeper of all time and is the only keeper to win the Ballon d'Or (1963).
The 'Ultras' are passionate organized fan groups common in European and South American soccer known for their intense support.
Lionel Messi joined Inter Miami in MLS in 2023, immediately becoming the most famous player in American soccer history.
The 'Aguerooo!' moment in 2012 saw Sergio Agüero score a last-second goal to win Manchester City's first Premier League title.
The Champions League anthem, created by Tony Britten and based on Handel's 'Zadok the Priest,' is one of the most recognizable pieces of sports music.
São Paulo's Maracanã Stadium has held the World Cup Final twice (1950 and 2014) and can seat nearly 80,000 fans.
The 'False 9' position, popularized by Guardiola with Messi at Barcelona, uses a forward who drops deep instead of staying as a traditional striker.
Robert Lewandowski scored 41 Bundesliga goals in a single season for Bayern Munich in 2020-21, breaking Gerd Müller's 49-year-old record.
The 'Gegenpressing' style of play, popularized by Jürgen Klopp, involves intense pressing immediately after losing the ball.
Andrea Pirlo's languid playing style and pinpoint passing earned him the nickname 'The Architect' and 'The Maestro.'
The Copa América is the oldest international continental football competition, first held in 1916.
Jack Grealish's low socks became a fashion statement in the Premier League, defying traditional kit norms.
Oliver Kahn was the last goalkeeper to win the Golden Ball award at a World Cup (2002).
The 'Goal Line Technology' system was introduced after a controversial goal in the 2010 World Cup where Frank Lampard's clear goal was disallowed.
Thierry Henry is Arsenal's all-time leading scorer with 228 goals and is regarded as one of the Premier League's greatest players.
The 'Treble' — winning the league, domestic cup, and Champions League in one season — has only been achieved by a handful of clubs.
Manchester City completed the Treble in 2023, only the second English club to achieve the feat after Manchester United in 1999.
Women's soccer has grown exponentially, with the 2023 Women's World Cup setting attendance records with over 1.9 million total spectators.
Alfredo Di Stéfano led Real Madrid to five consecutive European Cup titles from 1956 to 1960.
The 'Yellow Card' system was introduced in 1970, inspired by traffic lights, to clearly communicate cautions to players.
A 'Red Card' results in an immediate ejection, and the offending team must play with 10 players for the remainder of the match.
The Nike Mercurial, Adidas Predator, and Puma King are among the most iconic soccer boot lines in history.
Luis Suárez's biting incidents — three times in competitive matches — led to multiple lengthy bans.
Johan Cruyff never won a World Cup despite being one of the greatest players ever, with the Netherlands losing the 1974 final to West Germany.
The 'Crossbar Challenge' is a popular practice drill and social media trend where players try to hit the crossbar from distance.
Gerd Müller scored 365 goals in 427 Bundesliga appearances, earning him the nickname 'Der Bomber.'
The 'Nutmeg' — playing the ball through an opponent's legs — is one of soccer's most humiliating and entertaining moves.
Paolo Maldini played for AC Milan for 25 seasons, from 1985 to 2009, and is considered one of the greatest defenders ever.
Messi scored 91 goals in the 2012 calendar year, breaking Gerd Müller's record of 85 goals in a calendar year.
The 'Assist King' title goes to players like Mesut Özil, Xavi, and Kevin De Bruyne, who are known for their creative passing.
Ronaldinho's combination of skill, flair, and showmanship made him perhaps the most entertaining player to ever play soccer.
The 'Curved Free Kick' was perfected by players like Roberto Carlos, David Beckham, and Juninho Pernambucano.
Roberto Carlos's free kick against France in 1997, which bent dramatically before scoring, is considered the greatest free kick ever taken.
The 'Keeper Sweeper' role, as played by Manuel Neuer, involves the goalkeeper acting as an extra defender far from their goal.
The MLS has grown from 10 teams in 1996 to 30 teams by 2025, with several more expansion franchises planned.
Carli Lloyd scored a hat trick in the 2015 Women's World Cup Final, including a goal from the halfway line.
The 'Invincibles' refers to Arsenal's 2003-04 Premier League season, where they went the entire 38-game campaign unbeaten.
Wayne Rooney is Manchester United's all-time leading scorer with 253 goals in 559 appearances.
The 'North London Derby' between Arsenal and Tottenham is one of English football's fiercest local rivalries.
A 'Clean Sheet' means a goalkeeper and defense allowed zero goals in a match.
Xavi and Andrés Iniesta formed the greatest midfield partnership in soccer history at Barcelona and with Spain.
Jamie Vardy's remarkable journey from non-league football to Premier League champion with Leicester City in 2016 is one of sport's greatest stories.
Leicester City's 2015-16 Premier League title was a 5,000-to-1 odds fairy tale, considered one of the biggest upsets in sports history.
The 'Olimpico' goal is scored directly from a corner kick, named after the Argentine team that first achieved it in 1924.
Karim Benzema won the 2022 Ballon d'Or at age 34, ending Messi and Ronaldo's near-stranglehold on the award.
The 'Derby della Madonnina' between AC Milan and Inter Milan is one of Italian football's most passionate rivalries.
The 'Puskas Award' honors the most aesthetically pleasing goal scored each year and is named after Hungarian legend Ferenc Puskás.
N'Golo Kanté is widely regarded as one of soccer's most humble superstars, known for his relentless work rate and quiet personality.
Hope Solo is the most capped goalkeeper in U.S. women's soccer history with 202 international appearances.
The 'Fergie Time' phenomenon refers to Manchester United's tendency to score late goals during Sir Alex Ferguson's management era.
Sir Alex Ferguson managed Manchester United for 26 years (1986-2013), winning 38 trophies — the most successful manager in English football history.
The 'Transfer Window' system restricts when clubs can buy and sell players to specific periods, typically summer and January.
Wembley Stadium, the 'Home of Football,' was rebuilt in 2007 and seats 90,000, making it the largest stadium in the UK.
The phrase 'Park the Bus' describes an extremely defensive tactical approach, where all 10 outfield players defend near their own goal.
Jude Bellingham became one of soccer's biggest stars at Real Madrid, scoring goals at a remarkable rate for a midfielder.
The 'Rabona' goal by Erik Lamela for Tottenham in 2020 won the FIFA Puskás Award for best goal of the year.
Mohamed Salah broke the Premier League scoring record for a 38-game season with 32 goals in 2017-18.
Virgil van Dijk's arrival at Liverpool in 2018 transformed them from a leaky defense to Champions League winners.
The 'Viking Clap' celebration, popularized by Iceland's fans at Euro 2016, became a worldwide phenomenon.
Son Heung-min became the first Asian player to win the Premier League Golden Boot, scoring 23 goals in 2021-22.
The 2022 World Cup in Qatar was the first to be held in the Middle East and the first played in November-December.
David Beckham's free kick against Greece in 2001, which qualified England for the 2002 World Cup, is one of English football's greatest moments.
The 'Bielsa Bucket' became iconic when Argentine coach Marcelo Bielsa was seen sitting on an overturned bucket on the sidelines.
Pelé's real name was Edson Arantes do Nascimento — 'Pelé' was a childhood nickname with no clear meaning.
A 'Brace' in soccer means scoring two goals in a single match.
The Camp Nou in Barcelona can hold nearly 100,000 spectators, making it the largest soccer stadium in Europe.
Gabriel Batistuta, 'Batigol,' is Argentina's all-time leading Serie A scorer and known for his thunderous strikes.
The 'Winter Break' is a mid-season pause common in many European leagues, though the Premier League traditionally played through Christmas.
Didier Drogba is considered the greatest African player in Premier League history, scoring in four major cup finals.
The 'Tiki-Taka' style of play emphasized possession rates above 65%, with Spain and Barcelona perfecting the approach.
The 'Dark Arts' of soccer include time-wasting, simulation (diving), and on-field gamesmanship used to gain advantages.
George Best, the legendary Northern Irish winger, was known for his incredible dribbling and his quote: 'I spent a lot of money on booze, birds, and fast cars. The rest I just squandered.'
Pelé scored in four consecutive World Cups (1958, 1962, 1966, 1970).
The 'Knuckleball' free kick technique, used by Cristiano Ronaldo, involves striking the ball with no spin so it moves unpredictably.
Abby Wambach scored 184 international goals for the USWNT, a record at the time of her retirement in 2015.
The 'Superclásico' between Boca Juniors and River Plate in Argentina is considered one of the most intense derbies in world football.
Soccer's Law 17 governs corner kicks, which were introduced to the rules in 1872.
The 'Pressing Trap' is a defensive technique where a team intentionally allows a pass to one area then rapidly closes down that player.
Francesco Totti played 25 seasons for AS Roma, scoring 307 goals and becoming the club's all-time legend.
The Women's Super League (WSL) in England has grown rapidly, with matches now regularly broadcast on national television.
The 'Overlapping Run' involves a fullback running past a winger to create numerical superiority in wide areas.
Kaka was the last player other than Messi or Ronaldo to win the Ballon d'Or, winning it in 2007.
The first international soccer match was played between Scotland and England in 1872, ending in a 0-0 draw.
Kevin De Bruyne's 2022-23 pass completion and assist numbers are considered among the best midfield performances in Premier League history.
The 'Away Goals' rule was used in European competitions for decades before being abolished by UEFA in 2021.
Christian Pulisic became the first American to score in a Champions League Final when Chelsea won the 2021 title.
The 'Sombrero' flick sends the ball over an opponent's head using a deft chip — one of futsal's signature moves adopted in the full game.
Manchester City have dominated the Premier League since the 2017-18 season, winning six titles in seven years.
The 'Hand Ball' rule has been one of soccer's most contentious, particularly with the introduction of VAR.
Gianluigi Buffon played professional soccer for 28 years and is considered one of the greatest goalkeepers in history.
Tim Howard's 16-save performance for the USA against Belgium in the 2014 World Cup set a record for saves in a World Cup match.
Futsal — a 5-a-side indoor version of soccer — has produced many of the world's greatest technical players, including Ronaldinho and Messi.
The 'Financial Fair Play' (FFP) rules were introduced by UEFA in 2011 to prevent clubs from spending beyond their means.
Dennis Bergkamp's goal against Argentina in the 1998 World Cup is often cited as the greatest World Cup goal ever scored.
The 'Phenomenon' Ronaldo (R9) scored 15 World Cup goals across four tournaments for Brazil, including 8 in the 2002 tournament.
Sam Kerr became Australia's all-time leading scorer and one of the most prolific strikers in women's soccer history.
The 'Blaugrana' are Barcelona's club colors — blue and red (or garnet) — which have been worn since the club's founding in 1899.
Only 8 countries have ever won the FIFA Men's World Cup: Brazil, Germany, Italy, Argentina, France, Uruguay, England, and Spain.
The 'Match Ball' used in the World Cup is specially designed for each tournament — the 2022 ball was called 'Al Rihla' (The Journey).
Zinedine Zidane scored two goals in the 1998 World Cup Final, leading France to their first-ever World Cup title.
The 'Golden Boot' award goes to the top scorer at each FIFA World Cup tournament.
Just Fontaine holds the record for most goals in a single World Cup with 13 goals for France in 1958.
The 'Merseyside Derby' between Liverpool and Everton is one of the most-played derbies in English football history.
Gonzalo Higuain holds the record for most Serie A goals in a single season with 36 goals for Napoli in 2015-16.
The 'Calcio Storico' in Florence, Italy, is a historical form of football dating back to the 16th century, known for its brutality.
Luka Modrić won the 2018 Ballon d'Or after leading Croatia to the World Cup Final, breaking the Messi-Ronaldo duopoly.
The 'Tifosi' are the passionate fans of Italian soccer clubs, particularly those of Juventus, Inter, AC Milan, and Roma.
Pep Guardiola has won over 35 trophies as a manager across Barcelona, Bayern Munich, and Manchester City.
The 'Bosman Ruling' in 1995 allowed players to move freely at the end of their contracts, transforming the transfer market.
Antoine Griezmann's 'Hotline Bling' celebration, mimicking Drake's dance, became one of soccer's most recognizable celebrations.
The 'Number 10' shirt is traditionally worn by a team's playmaker and is associated with legends like Pelé, Maradona, Messi, and Zidane.
The 'Samba Style' of Brazilian soccer emphasizes individual flair, creativity, and joyful play.
Landon Donovan's stoppage-time goal against Algeria in the 2010 World Cup is one of the greatest moments in U.S. soccer history.
The average Premier League player earns about £3.4 million per year, though top stars earn significantly more.
The 'Supporter Shield' in MLS is awarded to the team with the best regular-season record, similar to league titles in Europe.
Andrés Iniesta's goal in the 2010 World Cup Final gave Spain its first-ever World Cup title.
The offside trap is a defensive strategy where defenders step forward simultaneously to catch attackers offside.
Soccer was included in the Olympic Games for men starting in 1900 and for women starting in 1996.
Old Trafford, home of Manchester United since 1910, is nicknamed 'The Theatre of Dreams' — a phrase coined by Bobby Charlton.
Anfield, Liverpool's home since 1892, is famous for its 'Kop' stand and the pre-match singing of 'You'll Never Walk Alone.'
The San Siro (officially Stadio Giuseppe Meazza) in Milan is shared by both AC Milan and Inter Milan and seats over 75,000.
Signal Iduna Park in Dortmund has the 'Yellow Wall' (Südtribüne), the largest standing terrace in European football with 25,000 capacity.
The Allianz Arena in Munich can change the color of its exterior to red (Bayern), blue (1860 Munich), or white (Germany national team).
Estadio Azteca in Mexico City is the only stadium to have hosted two FIFA World Cup Finals (1970 and 1986).
La Bombonera, home of Boca Juniors in Buenos Aires, is famous for literally shaking when fans jump and chant during matches.
Soccer City (FNB Stadium) in Johannesburg, shaped like a traditional African pot, hosted the 2010 World Cup Final with 84,490 capacity.
The Santiago Bernabéu underwent a massive €1.85 billion renovation completed in 2024, adding a retractable roof and 360-degree video screen.
The Tottenham Hotspur Stadium, opened in 2019, features the world's first dividing retractable football pitch, with an NFL field underneath.
Emirates Stadium became Arsenal's home in 2006 when they moved from their historic Highbury ground, increasing capacity from 38,419 to 60,704.
Stamford Bridge has been Chelsea's home since 1905 and sits in the affluent Fulham area of London with a capacity of about 40,341.
The Etihad Stadium became Manchester City's home in 2003, originally built for the 2002 Commonwealth Games.
Estadio da Luz in Lisbon, home of Benfica, seats 64,642 and means 'Stadium of Light' — named after the parish of Luz.
Celtic Park in Glasgow, known as 'Paradise,' holds 60,411 fans and is the largest football stadium in Scotland.
The Johan Cruyff Arena (formerly Amsterdam Arena), home of Ajax, was one of Europe's first stadiums with a retractable roof when built in 1996.
The Stadio Olimpico in Rome is shared by AS Roma and SS Lazio, creating one of Italian football's most intense shared-ground rivalries.
The Stadio Diego Armando Maradona in Naples (formerly San Paolo) was renamed in 2020 after the death of Diego Maradona.
Parc des Princes has been Paris Saint-Germain's home since 1974 and seats 47,929 in the 16th arrondissement of Paris.
Estadio Monumental Antonio Vespucio Liberti in Buenos Aires is River Plate's home and Argentina's largest stadium at 84,567 capacity.
Estadio Centenario in Montevideo, Uruguay, hosted the first-ever World Cup Final in 1930 and is a FIFA World Football Monument.
The Rose Bowl in Pasadena, California, hosted the 1994 World Cup Final and the 1999 Women's World Cup Final.
Lusail Stadium in Qatar, built specifically for the 2022 World Cup Final, has a golden, bowl-shaped design inspired by Arab lanterns.
The Stade de France in Saint-Denis, Paris, was built for the 1998 World Cup and has a capacity of 80,698.
Olympiastadion in Berlin hosted the 2006 World Cup Final between Italy and France and was originally built for the 1936 Olympics.
The Millennium Stadium (Principality Stadium) in Cardiff has a retractable roof and hosted the 2017 Champions League Final.
Ataturk Olympic Stadium in Istanbul hosted the legendary 2005 Champions League Final — Liverpool's 'Miracle of Istanbul' comeback.
Hampden Park in Glasgow is one of the oldest football stadiums in the world, first used for football in 1903.
The Metropolitano Stadium in Madrid, home of Atlético Madrid since 2017, replaced their legendary Vicente Calderón.
San Mamés in Bilbao, home of Athletic Club, is called 'The Cathedral' and the club famously only signs Basque players.
Real Madrid's 'Real' means 'Royal' in Spanish — the title was bestowed by King Alfonso XIII in 1920.
FC Barcelona was founded in 1899 by Swiss businessman Joan Gamper, with the club's motto 'Més que un club' (More than a club).
Manchester United were originally called Newton Heath LYR Football Club, founded in 1878 by railway workers, renamed in 1902.
Arsenal were originally called 'Royal Arsenal,' founded in 1886 by munitions workers in Woolwich, southeast London, before moving to north London in 1913.
Chelsea FC was founded in 1905 and named after the London borough, though the ground is actually in neighboring Fulham.
Tottenham Hotspur were named after Sir Henry Percy ('Harry Hotspur'), a medieval nobleman — the cockerel on their badge represents his fighting spurs.
Liverpool FC was founded in 1892 after a dispute between Everton and their Anfield landlord John Houlding, who started a new club at the ground.
Juventus, meaning 'youth' in Latin, was founded by school students in Turin in 1897 — their black and white stripes were adopted in 1903.
AC Milan was founded in 1899 by English expatriates, which is why the club uses the English spelling 'Milan' rather than Italian 'Milano.'
Inter Milan (FC Internazionale) was formed in 1908 by breakaway members of AC Milan who wanted to include international players.
Bayern Munich was founded on February 27, 1900, by eleven football players led by Franz John at a restaurant in Munich.
Borussia Dortmund's 'Borussia' is the Latin name for Prussia — the club was founded in 1909 by young beer drinkers at a pub.
Paris Saint-Germain was formed in 1970 through a merger, making it relatively young compared to other European giants.
Ajax Amsterdam is named after the Greek mythological hero Ajax — their logo depicts a portrait of the warrior.
Celtic FC was founded in 1887 by Irish Marist Brother Walfrid to raise funds for the poor in Glasgow's East End Irish immigrant community.
Rangers FC was founded in 1872, making it one of the world's oldest football clubs, and has won a record 55 Scottish league titles.
Benfica's full name is Sport Lisboa e Benfica — the eagle on their crest symbolizes independence and authority.
Boca Juniors chose their colors (blue with a yellow horizontal band) from the flag of the next ship to arrive at the port of Buenos Aires — a Swedish vessel.
Santos FC, founded in 1912 in the port city of Santos, became world-famous as the club where Pelé played from 1956 to 1974.
Flamengo is Brazil's most popular club with an estimated 40 million fans — their name comes from a neighborhood in Rio de Janeiro.
Atlético Madrid's red-and-white stripes were adopted because the club's founders bought cheap mattress fabric for their shirts in the early 1900s.
Wolverhampton Wanderers ('Wolves') were one of England's founding football clubs and pioneers of European competition in the 1950s.
Everton FC, founded in 1878, are nicknamed 'The Toffees' — possibly after a toffee shop near their original ground.
West Ham United are nicknamed 'The Hammers' due to their origins as Thames Ironworks FC, founded by shipyard workers in 1895.
AS Roma's badge features a she-wolf suckling Romulus and Remus, referencing the mythological founding of Rome.
Napoli was founded in 1926 and play in sky blue, a color associated with the city of Naples and the Mediterranean Sea.
Marseille's Olympique de Marseille (OM) is France's most popular club and the first French team to win the Champions League (1993).
Feyenoord from Rotterdam were the first Dutch club to win the European Cup in 1970, before Ajax's famous hat trick of titles.
The LA Galaxy were the first major MLS club and have won a record 5 MLS Cup titles. They signed David Beckham in 2007, transforming American soccer.
FC St. Pauli in Hamburg is famous for its skull-and-crossbones flag and left-wing, anti-establishment fan culture unique in world football.
The 1934 World Cup in Italy was heavily influenced by Benito Mussolini, who used the tournament as political propaganda for his fascist regime.
The 1938 World Cup in France was the last tournament before World War II — the competition didn't resume until 1950.
The 1954 World Cup featured the 'Miracle of Berne' — West Germany defeated heavily favored Hungary 3-2 in the final despite Hungary being unbeaten for four years.
The 1958 World Cup in Sweden saw a 17-year-old Pelé burst onto the world stage, scoring six goals including two in the final.
The 1962 World Cup in Chile featured the 'Battle of Santiago,' a notoriously violent match between Chile and Italy that required police intervention.
The 1966 World Cup Final — England 4, West Germany 2 — featured Geoff Hurst's controversial third goal and Kenneth Wolstenholme's iconic commentary: 'They think it's all over... it is now!'
The 1970 World Cup in Mexico is considered the greatest tournament ever, with Brazil's legendary team playing the most beautiful football in World Cup history.
The 1974 World Cup introduced the Netherlands' 'Total Football' philosophy to the world, though they lost the final to West Germany.
The 1978 World Cup in Argentina was controversial due to the military dictatorship — the tournament was surrounded by political tension and allegations of match-fixing.
The 1982 World Cup in Spain featured Paolo Rossi's incredible comeback from a match-fixing ban to lead Italy to victory with six goals.
The 1986 World Cup was moved from Colombia to Mexico due to financial difficulties — it became Maradona's tournament with his two iconic goals against England.
The 1990 World Cup in Italy was famously defensive and low-scoring, with Cameroon's shock opening-day defeat of defending champions Argentina.
The 1994 World Cup in the USA saw Roberto Baggio miss the decisive penalty in the final, sending Brazil to their fourth World Cup title.
The 1998 World Cup in France featured host nation France winning their first World Cup, led by Zinedine Zidane's two goals in the final.
The 2002 World Cup, co-hosted by South Korea and Japan, was the first in Asia and featured stunning upsets — South Korea reached the semifinals.
The 2006 World Cup in Germany is remembered for the dramatic final between Italy and France, ended by Zidane's headbutt on Materazzi and Italy winning on penalties.
The 2010 World Cup in South Africa was the first on African soil - famous for the vuvuzela horns, Paul the Octopus, and Spain winning their first-ever World Cup.
The 2014 World Cup in Brazil featured Germany's astonishing 7-1 demolition of hosts Brazil in the semifinal — known as the 'Mineirazo.'
The 2018 World Cup in Russia saw France win their second title, with Mbappé (19) becoming the youngest player since Pelé to score in a final.
The 2022 World Cup Final between Argentina and France is considered the greatest final ever — Messi and Mbappé each scored twice before Argentina won on penalties.
The FIFA World Cup trophy is made of 18-carat gold, weighs 13.61 pounds, and depicts two human figures holding up the Earth.
The original World Cup trophy, the Jules Rimet Trophy, was permanently awarded to Brazil after their third win in 1970 and was stolen in 1983, never recovered.
Miroslav Klose holds the all-time World Cup scoring record with 16 goals across four tournaments (2002-2014) for Germany.
Franz Beckenbauer, 'Der Kaiser,' revolutionized the sweeper position and is the only person to win the World Cup as both captain (1974) and manager (1990).
Eusébio, the 'Black Panther' from Mozambique, scored 733 goals in 745 matches for Benfica and Portugal and was the 1966 World Cup's top scorer with 9 goals.
Garrincha, nicknamed 'The Joy of the People,' had legs of different lengths due to childhood polio but became one of Brazil's greatest ever wingers.
Zico, known as the 'White Pelé,' scored 52 goals in 71 appearances for Brazil and is considered one of the greatest playmakers in football history.
Michel Platini scored a stunning 9 goals in 5 games at Euro 1984, leading France to the European Championship — a tournament record that still stands.
Bobby Charlton survived the 1958 Munich air disaster that killed 8 Manchester United players and went on to lead England to their only World Cup in 1966.
Ferenc Puskás scored 84 goals in 85 international matches for Hungary and later scored 242 goals in 262 matches for Real Madrid.
Marco van Basten's volleyed goal in the 1988 European Championship Final is widely considered the greatest goal in tournament history.
Ruud Gullit helped define the great AC Milan side of the late 1980s and was one of the first major Black European superstars in football.
Franco Baresi spent his entire 20-year career (1977-1997) at AC Milan and is considered one of the greatest defenders in football history — Milan retired his number 6.
Roberto Baggio, 'The Divine Ponytail,' won the 1993 Ballon d'Or and is remembered for both his brilliance and his tragic penalty miss in the 1994 World Cup Final.
Rivaldo won the Ballon d'Or in 1999 and scored a famous overhead kick hat-trick goal against Valencia in 2001 to keep Barcelona in the Champions League.
Romário scored over 1,000 career goals and was the 1994 World Cup's best player — his clinical finishing helped Brazil win their fourth title.
Cafu is the only player to appear in three consecutive World Cup Finals (1994, 1998, 2002), winning twice with Brazil.
Raúl scored 323 goals for Real Madrid over 16 seasons and was the Champions League's all-time top scorer until Messi and Ronaldo surpassed him.
Samuel Eto'o won three Champions League titles (two with Barcelona, one with Inter Milan) and is considered the greatest African player in Champions League history.
Andriy Shevchenko won the 2004 Ballon d'Or after scoring 175 goals in 296 appearances for AC Milan, becoming Ukraine's greatest-ever footballer.
Alessandro Del Piero scored 290 goals in 705 appearances for Juventus, becoming their all-time leading scorer and a club legend.
Pavel Nedvěd's tireless energy and powerful left foot earned him the 2003 Ballon d'Or while playing for Juventus.
Luís Figo's controversial transfer from Barcelona to Real Madrid in 2000 for a world-record fee made him one of football's most polarizing figures.
Sócrates was a Brazilian midfielder who was also a medical doctor — he captained the legendary 1982 Brazil World Cup team and was a political activist.
Lothar Matthäus holds the record for most World Cup appearances (25 matches across five tournaments) and won the first FIFA World Player of the Year in 1991.
George Weah is the only African player to win the Ballon d'Or (1995) and later became the President of Liberia in 2018.
Davor Šuker won the Golden Boot at the 1998 World Cup with 6 goals for Croatia in their first-ever World Cup appearance as an independent nation.
Hristo Stoichkov, the Bulgarian star, won the 1994 Ballon d'Or after leading Bulgaria to an improbable World Cup semifinal.
Dino Zoff remains the oldest player to win a World Cup — he captained Italy to victory at age 40 in 1982.
Gordon Banks' save from Pelé's header in the 1970 World Cup is widely regarded as the greatest save in football history.
Kenny Dalglish is the only player to score over 100 goals in both English and Scottish top-flight football (Liverpool and Celtic).
Eric Cantona's kung-fu kick on a Crystal Palace fan in 1995 led to a nine-month ban, but he returned to lead Manchester United to the title.
Just Fontaine's 13 goals at the 1958 World Cup set an all-time record for a single tournament that has never been remotely threatened.
Hungarian great Sándor Kocsis scored 75 goals in 68 internationals and had an astounding 11 goals at the 1954 World Cup.
Bobby Moore captained England to their 1966 World Cup victory and is considered the finest English defender of all time — a statue stands at Wembley.
Stanley Matthews, the 'Wizard of Dribble,' won the first-ever Ballon d'Or in 1956 at age 41 — the oldest recipient in history.
Dennis Law is the only Scottish footballer to win the Ballon d'Or (1964) and remains one of Manchester United's greatest-ever strikers.
Raymond Kopa, the French-Polish forward, won three consecutive European Cups with Real Madrid and the 1958 Ballon d'Or.
Omar Sívori won the 1961 Ballon d'Or playing for Juventus — born in Argentina, he also represented Italy internationally.
Giacinto Facchetti revolutionized the fullback position in the 1960s, scoring 75 goals from defense for Inter Milan — extraordinary for a defender.
Gianfranco Zola, Chelsea's greatest foreign player, won the FWA Footballer of the Year in 1997 and had a statue erected outside Stamford Bridge.
David Beckham popularized soccer in America and became one of sport's biggest global brands — his free kicks and crossing ability were unmatched.
Ronaldo 'Il Fenomeno' (R9) won the World Cup twice (1994, 2002), two Ballon d'Ors, and is regarded as one of the greatest pure strikers ever.
Vinícius Júnior has become Real Madrid's most dangerous attacker, winning the Champions League in 2024 and finishing as a top Ballon d'Or contender.
Pedri, Barcelona's young Spanish midfielder, has been compared to Andrés Iniesta for his vision, composure, and ability to dictate play.
Gavi became the youngest-ever Kopa Trophy winner in 2022, awarded to the best player under 21.
Bukayo Saka has become Arsenal's most important player, named the club's Player of the Season multiple times and a key England international.
Phil Foden, a product of Manchester City's academy, won the PFA Young Player of the Year multiple times and became England's next great creative talent.
Cole Palmer emerged as Chelsea's star player after leaving Manchester City, winning the PFA Young Player of the Year in 2024.
Lamine Yamal became the youngest player ever to play and score at a European Championship at 16, starring for Spain at Euro 2024.
Florian Wirtz of Bayer Leverkusen has been hailed as Germany's most talented young prospect, helping Leverkusen win an unbeaten Bundesliga title in 2024.
Victor Osimhen's 26 Serie A goals in 2022-23 fired Napoli to their first Scudetto in 33 years, ending their long wait since the Maradona era.
Rodri won the 2024 Ballon d'Or after a stellar season with Manchester City and a decisive performance for Spain at Euro 2024.
Alisson Becker is widely regarded as the best goalkeeper in the world, transforming Liverpool's defense after joining from Roma in 2018.
Thibaut Courtois made a stunning return from injury to star for Real Madrid, having made match-winning saves in the 2022 Champions League Final.
Harry Kane left Tottenham for Bayern Munich in 2023, quickly becoming the Bundesliga's most prolific foreign scorer.
Declan Rice's £105 million transfer from West Ham to Arsenal in 2023 made him the most expensive British player of all time.
Federico Valverde has become one of the world's most complete midfielders at Real Madrid, combining technique, power, and tireless running.
Rafael Leão's explosive pace and dribbling at AC Milan have made the Portuguese winger one of Serie A's most exciting players.
Martin Ødegaard captains Arsenal despite being just 25, having fulfilled the promise that saw Real Madrid sign him at age 16.
Jamal Musiala chose to represent Germany over England and has become Bayern Munich's most creative force and one of football's brightest young talents.
Aurélien Tchouaméni anchors Real Madrid's midfield and is considered France's long-term successor to the holding midfielder role.
Enzo Fernández won the 2022 World Cup Young Player award with Argentina and then joined Chelsea for a British-record £106.8 million.
William Saliba's partnership with Gabriel at Arsenal's center-back formed one of the best defensive duos in Premier League history.
Alejandro Garnacho has emerged as an exciting young talent at Manchester United, with his overhead kick goal winning multiple awards.
Real Madrid have won a record 15 Champions League/European Cup titles — more than any other club — including six in the 21st century alone.
Barcelona's 2009 team under Pep Guardiola won an unprecedented six trophies in a single calendar year (the Sextuple).
Bayern Munich have won over 30 Bundesliga titles and 6 European Cups/Champions League trophies, dominating German football for decades.
Juventus have won a record 36 Serie A titles and are Italy's most successful club, earning the nickname 'La Vecchia Signora' (The Old Lady).
Liverpool's five European Cup/Champions League victories include the improbable 2005 comeback and their dominant 2019 squad under Jürgen Klopp.
Manchester United's 'Class of '92' — Beckham, Giggs, Scholes, Neville brothers, and Butt — emerged from the academy to form a dynasty under Sir Alex Ferguson.
Manchester City were a mid-table club before their 2008 takeover by Abu Dhabi's Sheikh Mansour transformed them into serial Premier League winners.
Chelsea's modern era began with Roman Abramovich's purchase in 2003, financing an era that produced 5 Premier League titles and 2 Champions League wins.
Ajax's legendary 1970s team won three consecutive European Cups (1971-73) and produced Cruyff, Neeskens, and Rep.
Benfica won back-to-back European Cups in 1961 and 1962 with Eusébio, establishing themselves as one of Portugal's greatest clubs.
Porto defied the odds to win the 2004 Champions League under José Mourinho, launching his career as one of football's most famous managers.
The Old Firm derby between Celtic and Rangers in Glasgow is one of the most intense rivalries in world football, rooted in religious and cultural divisions.
Nottingham Forest won back-to-back European Cups in 1979 and 1980 under Brian Clough, having been promoted from the Second Division just two years earlier.
Red Star Belgrade won the 1991 European Cup, becoming the only Serbian club to win Europe's top prize.
Steaua Bucharest (now FCSB) won the 1986 European Cup, making them the first Eastern European club to win the tournament.
Club América is Mexico's most successful and controversial club with 13 Liga MX titles, beloved and hated in equal measure across Mexico.
River Plate and Boca Juniors' rivalry — the Superclásico — divides Argentina along class lines: River represents the upper class, Boca the working class.
Al-Hilal and Al-Ahli in Saudi Arabia have become global talking points after signing stars like Neymar, Benzema, and other European superstars.
The European Championship (Euros) has been held since 1960 — Germany and Spain are tied for the most wins with 3 titles each.
Greece's Euro 2004 victory is considered the greatest upset in European Championship history — they were 150-1 outsiders.
Denmark won Euro 1992 despite not originally qualifying — they replaced Yugoslavia (banned due to civil war) and stunned the continent.
The Africa Cup of Nations (AFCON) is the continent's premier tournament, first held in 1957 with only three teams — Egypt, Sudan, and Ethiopia.
Egypt has won the Africa Cup of Nations a record 7 times, while Cameroon (5 titles) are known as the 'Indomitable Lions.'
The Copa Libertadores is South America's equivalent of the Champions League — Independiente of Argentina have won it a record 7 times.
The Asian Cup, first held in 1956, has seen Japan win 4 times — the most of any nation in the competition.
The CONCACAF Gold Cup is the top national team tournament for North America, Central America, and the Caribbean — Mexico leads with 12 wins.
The FIFA Club World Cup expanded from 7 teams to 32 teams in 2025, creating a month-long tournament modeled after the World Cup.
The Women's European Championship (Women's Euros) saw England win the 2022 tournament on home soil before 87,192 fans at Wembley.
The NWSL (National Women's Soccer League) in the USA has grown rapidly since its 2013 founding, attracting record crowds and major investment.
Ada Hegerberg of Norway won the first-ever Women's Ballon d'Or in 2018 and is the all-time Champions League top scorer.
Mia Hamm scored 158 international goals for the USWNT and was the face of women's soccer worldwide in the 1990s and early 2000s.
Christine Sinclair retired as the all-time leading international scorer (men's or women's) with 190 goals for Canada.
Alex Popp, Germany's veteran striker, has been a symbol of German women's football for over a decade.
The 1999 Women's World Cup Final at the Rose Bowl drew 90,185 fans — a record for any women's sporting event at the time.
Brandi Chastain's penalty kick and iconic jersey celebration in the 1999 Women's World Cup Final became one of the most famous images in sports.
Spain won their first-ever Women's World Cup in 2023, defeating England 1-0 in the final in Sydney, Australia.
Aitana Bonmatí won both the Women's Ballon d'Or and the Golden Ball at the 2023 Women's World Cup, establishing herself as the best player in the world.
Title IX in the United States (1972) dramatically increased women's participation in sports and is a major reason the US dominates women's soccer.
The 'Centurion' season — when a team reaches 100 points — was first achieved in a major European league by Manchester City in the 2017-18 Premier League.
The World Cup trophy cannot be permanently kept by any nation — the current trophy was introduced in 1974 and remains FIFA's property.
Soccer is called 'football' in most of the world — the word 'soccer' originated in England as slang for 'association football' in the 1880s.
A 'Cap' in international football refers to an appearance — the term comes from the tradition of giving players a physical cap for each game.
The 'Aguero moment' in 2012 — Manchester City's last-second title win — has been replayed more than any other Premier League moment.
The 'Double' (league and cup in one season) was first achieved in England by Preston North End in the 1888-89 season.
The 'Quadruple' — winning league, Champions League, FA Cup, and League Cup in one season — has never been achieved by an English club.
The Puskas Award for best goal of the year was first given in 2009 — Cristiano Ronaldo won the inaugural award for a goal against Porto.
Soccer's 'Laws of the Game' are maintained by the International Football Association Board (IFAB), which has existed since 1886.
The 'Wall' in free kicks must stand at least 9.15 meters (10 yards) from the ball.
The penalty spot is exactly 12 yards (11 meters) from the goal line, a distance that has remained unchanged since 1891.
Extra time (two 15-minute halves) and penalty shootouts were first used at the 1970 World Cup to decide knockout matches.
The 'Advantage Rule' allows the referee to let play continue after a foul if the team in possession would benefit more from continuing than receiving a free kick.
Substitutions were first allowed in the World Cup in 1970 — originally only two per match, now five are permitted following COVID-era rule changes.
The FIFA World Rankings, introduced in 1992, determine tournament seedings and are updated monthly based on match results.
VAR (Video Assistant Referee) reviews four types of decisions: goals, penalties, direct red cards, and mistaken identity.
The longest unbeaten run in international football belongs to Brazil, who went 35 games without defeat from 1993 to 1996.
In professional soccer, the pitch can legally be any length between 100-110 meters and any width between 64-75 meters.
The 'Diagonal System of Control' is the standard method of referee positioning, with the referee running diagonally across the pitch.
The '12th Man' refers to a team's fans — Fenerbahçe's supporters once generated 131.76 decibels, a Guinness World Record for stadium noise.
Transfer deadline day has become a media spectacle in England, with live coverage outside training grounds as clubs rush to complete deals.
The Premier League's international broadcast rights were worth over £5 billion for the 2022-25 cycle, making it the richest league in the world.
Soccer balls were originally made from inflated pig bladders wrapped in leather — modern balls use synthetic materials and are precisely manufactured.
The 'Ballon d'Or' ceremony is held annually in Paris at the Théâtre du Châtelet, organized by France Football magazine.
The first ever floodlit football match was played at Bramall Lane, Sheffield, in 1878 using lights powered by dynamos.
The 'Inverted Fullback' position, popularized by Pep Guardiola at Manchester City, sees fullbacks tuck inside to form a midfield double pivot.
Saudi Arabia's shock 2-1 victory over Argentina in the 2022 World Cup group stage is one of the greatest upsets in World Cup history.
Senegal's 1-0 upset of defending champions France in the opening match of the 2002 World Cup stunned the football world.
North Korea shocked Italy 1-0 at the 1966 World Cup, one of the biggest upsets in tournament history.
The USA shocked England 1-0 at the 1950 World Cup in Brazil, one of the most unlikely results in football history — most American players were amateurs.
